texto: Writing a novel is like taking a long cross-country journey. The hardest part is getting going, making sure you have all the items you need to take with you, double- and triple-checking that the route you’re taking is the best way. So often you leave your driveway and start north when you realize you actually needed to head southwest. I’ve never written a novel without a certain number of false starts. And it never seems to get easier. Part of me thinks it only gets harder.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Travis Thrasher: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de limites e escolhas conscientes. O trecho “Writing a novel is like taking a long cross-country journey. The hardest part is getting going, m…” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que reflexao, writing-process, novel pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de viagem e reflexao.

frase_original: Writing a novel is like taking a long cross-country journey. The hardest part is getting going, making sure you have all the items you need to take with you, double- and triple-checking that the route you’re taking is the best way. So often you leave your driveway and start north when you realize you actually needed to head southwest. I’ve never written a novel without a certain number of false starts. And it never seems to get easier. Part of me thinks it only gets harder.
